---
title: "Unit 6 In Class Discussion"
author:
  - Elizabeth King
  - Kevin Middleton
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, custom.scss]
    standalone: true
    self-contained: true
    logo: QMLS_Logo.png
    slide-number: true
    show-slide-number: all
code-annotations: hover
bibliography: QMLS_Bibliography.bib
csl: evolution.csl
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| message: false
#| warning: false

library(tidyverse)
library(cowplot)
library(performance)
library(see)

theme_set(theme_cowplot(font_size = 20))
```


## Today

- Reminder: look at the keys
- QMLS2 is scheduled for Spring 2027
- Coming up next units: more complex cases (more than 2 levels, multilevel, etc.)
- Questions
- Problem set

## Diagnostics 

- When is bad too bad?

## Assumptions of OLS

-  At each $X$, there is a normally distributed population of $Y$ observations with a mean at the regression line
-  The variance of all $Y$ observations is equal across the range of $X$. 

![](https://i.imgur.com/F0Y5Fva.png){fig-align="center" width=50%}

## Problematic residuals

![](https://openintro-ims.netlify.app/inf-model-slr_files/figure-html/fig-whatCanGoWrongWithLinearModel-1.png){fig-align="center" width=100%}

From [Introduction to Modern Statistics](https://openintro-ims.netlify.app/) (Çetinkaya-Rundel and Hardin, 2021)

## Quizzes

- 6-3
- 6-4

> Can you explain quiz question 6.3 question 2? How does the difference of means work to determine if something is greater than the other? I am assuming you are subtracting the two from each other, but how do you infer what it means beyond that?


## Influential points

> I would like to know more about when to exclude extreme values. When looking at influential observations (lecture 06-4, slide 16), I wonder what to do if there are points outside of the contour lines? Could this tell you that a point is too extreme and shouldn't be considered? Or do you need to make your own judgement call? 

## Approaches to Influential Points/Extreme Values

- Figure out what is going on
    - I don't know what is going on is an ok conclusion
- Detecting (be wary of arbitrary decision thresholds)
    - Leverage
    - Cook's Distance
- Determine the impact on parameter estimates
    - Leave out and compare

## Transformations

- When to transform or not?
    - To satisfy the assumptions of your model

> ... And when is it ethical to modify the data to fit a model and when does a different model need to be used? 

## Alternate statistical approaches

- Transformation (this class)
- Randomization (QMLS 2)
- Bayesian inference (QMLS 2)
- Robust regression

We do not recommend non-parametric statistics.

## Diagnostics 

- When is bad too bad?
- Reject black & white thinking
- Experiment to learn


## Priors and Posteriors {.smaller}

> I understand that you can use priors when modeling with the Bayesian framework. On slide slide 43 in video 06-2, it is mentioned that sigma is half-normal, from 0 to 1. Whole normal would be -1 to 1? But since we have prior information, negatives are unnecessary (i.e., not logical)? 

> I am a bit confused on how to check if our observed means are similar to the expected means based on our data. Is that the post hoc tests? Is this taking the information from our data set to create this or is it based off of the parameters we set to begin with. Since if it uses the data you are already plotting, wouldn't you just get a positive feedback loop of "oh yes here is my data and I'm going to use this to confirm what I see is there"


## Categorical vs continuous {.smaller}

> Is it ever beneficial to create multiple models where for instance in one you read "color" as categorical data and in another you read it as continuous (using a munsell color chart for example)? Is this just purely dependent on the research question or would reading certain "interchangeable" data types as categorical rather than continuous cause other issues I maybe haven't thought of?

> I'm curious about when to model Year as a categorical vs continous predictor. In the data I work with (calf weaning weight), I use it as a categorical because individuals are not repeated across years, and there is a different main predictor of interest in the model. The goal of year is to encompass one time events like a drought, sickness, etc. and not a progressive change. I'd be curious if you have thoughts or a rule of thumb, or if it's largely field specific. 

## Understanding model output: today!

> When we run a linear model on comparing two means, what exactly does the output give us? For instance, the process of using maximum likelihood and how that leads to the linear function model was very clear for the bivariate regression. However, for comparing two means, I don't understand. What does the output tell us here? What mean is most likely to give us the observations we got given the number of observations and our estimated mean?
